data.title: Can Apple's Services Segment Offset iPhone Weakness? (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has surged 34% since our April “Buy” rating, outperforming the broader market. Since our last coverage in August, AAPL’s performance has been relatively muted, with a slight increase of just 2%. Bullish momentum remains strong, with solid volume and technical support indicating further short-term upside, supporting our cautious buy rating with an underweight position.

Fundamentally, Apple’s expanding services segment and deepening ecosystem, lock-in drive higher-margin, recurring revenues, positioning the company for sustained long-term growth. However, risks remain with the declining share of iPhone revenue, market saturation, and longer upgrade cycles, which could dampen overall top-line growth despite solid performance in other areas.

Apple’s Bullish Surge: VPT Signals Upside to $250 and Beyond

AAPL is experiencing solid bullish momentum regarding the volume price trend (VPT). The VPT at 3.858 billion is over the annual moving average of 3.824 billion, suggesting a prolonged bullish sentiment for AAPL in the street. Considering the RSI, it is taking constant support over 60, suggesting that the stock holds short-term upside potential even near overbought levels.

Following the mid-term trends and 3-point Fibonacci levels, Apple stock may hit an average of $250 by the end of 2024 (at 1.0 Fibonacci level). Optimistically, the stock may reach $276 by the year’s end, which aligns with the 1.618 Fibonacci level. Pessimistically, the stock may end up ~$224 to hit the 0.5 Fibonacci level.

Finally, based on 44 years of monthly seasonality, the stock has a 70% probability of delivering a positive return. This points to a historical upside potential integrated into the stock prices.

Solid Services Segment Growth and Prolonged Ecosystem Lock-in

Apple has delivered and holds a fundamental capacity for business growth through its market presence, growing customer base, and high levels of customer satisfaction. As an emerging core fundamental growth element, the services segment of Apple had high growth in Q3 and 9M of fiscal 2024 against the same periods in 2023.

This growth is attributed primarily to increased net sales from advertising, the App Store, and cloud services. Services revenue as a percentage of total net sales has grown, increasing recurring revenue streams as part of Apple’s total revenue composition. Specifically, in Q3 2024, services revenue marked 28.23% of total net sales, up from 25.93% in Q3 2023. For 9M of 2024, services revenue led 24.04% to total net sales, against 21.41% in 9M 2023.

Moreover, this rising proportion of services revenue is vital because it suggests a shift in Apple’s business model towards higher-margin, recurring revenues. Such growth in the services may maintain its profitability and cash flow stability to balance the fluctuations in hardware sales. Year-over-year (YoY) growth in services revenue for Q3 2024 stands at 14.14% over 13.22% in Q3 2023. This acceleration in growth points to a robust demand for Apple’s ecosystem based on customer engagement across its installed base of devices. Developed and emerging markets support the record-setting services revenue of $24.2 billion in Q3 2024.

Apple’s ecosystem growth may continue with its expanding installed base across all product categories and geographic segments. This ecosystem lock-in effect (customers remain within Apple’s ecosystem due to the interdependence of hardware and services) enhances Apple’s ability to monetize its user base through service revenue growth.

Further, the iPhone still holds a dominance in the global smartphone industry. As of 2024, the iPhone holds a 27.73% market share in the industry, which provides Apple with a massive platform for growth through additional device sales and cross-selling services. Similarly, the number of iPhone users has grown substantially over the years. There are (estimated) 1.56 billion users in 2024 and over 1.46 billion in 2023, marking an increase of ~6.85% YoY. This steady increase points to a stable demand and high customer retention rates, providing a solid base for long-term service revenue growth.

In Q3, the growing iPhone installed base was strengthened by geographic expansion with record sales in countries like the UK, Spain, Poland, Mexico, Indonesia, and the Philippines. This reach highlights Apple’s capability to penetrate both developed and emerging markets. The high levels of customer satisfaction, with 98% for iPhone users in the US, may support Apple’s low churn rate and reinforce customer loyalty. This satisfaction ensures that existing users are more likely to upgrade within the Apple ecosystem, deriving more in recurring sales.

Moreover, Apple’s services segment is growing its profitability with a gross margin of 74% in Q3 against 70.5% in Q3 2023. This improvement in the bottom line and Apple’s ability to scale its services business without a commensurate rise in costs. Over 9M of 2024, the services gross margin stood at 73.8%, over 70.8% in 9M 2023. The improvement in service profitability reflects Apple’s capability to capitalize on its vast user base with high-margin digital services.

Looking forward, Apple’s strong earnings and revenue performance may persist with the progress of the service segment. In Q3, Apple derived $85.78 billion in total revenue, surpassing market expectations by $1.4 billion. This revenue beat may repeat in the upcoming Q4 2024 report, considering the top-line trend in the service segment. As per consensus estimates, Q4 revenue may hit $94.23 billion (with 21 upward revisions for revenue and three downward revisions over the last three months), signaling continued strong performance as the year progresses.

Dependence on iPhone Sales and Slowing Revenue Contribution

Apple’s heavy reliance on iPhone sales is becoming a downside for its consolidated top-line growth. The share of iPhone revenue as a percentage of total net sales has been declining over the past few years. For Q3 fiscal 2024, iPhone revenue constituted 45.81% of total net sales, down from 48.50% in Q3 2023. For 9M 2024, iPhone sales contributed 52.33% of total net sales, which declined from 53.36% in 9M 2023.

The proportion is concerning. Why? It signals Apple’s major reliance on the iPhone to derive top-line growth. While the company has tried to diversify its product portfolio, the iPhone remains a core product, and its shrinking contribution to total sales indicates a potential ceiling on product-based growth.

Finally, a YoY decline in iPhone revenue of 0.94% for Q3 and 1.16% for 9M highlights the weakening trajectory of this core product. Apple relied on iPhone upgrades and sales cycles to drive revenue. This reduction in its contribution could stifle top-line growth while competitors intensify their efforts in the smartphone market.

In short, AAPL may face downside pressure in the upcoming quarter as iPhone revenue is declining as a proportion of total sales with a faltering YoY growth. This drop is emerging from the saturation of smartphone markets, the lengthening of replacement cycles, and increased competition. Overall, the shrinking revenue across multiple quarters, despite the positive bump in 2024, suggests that overall consumer demand for Apple’s products may be slowing down.

Takeaway

Strong volume trends and solid technical support suggest a short-term upside, with potential targets of $250-$276 by year-end. Apple’s services segment continues to grow, driving higher-margin, recurring revenue and bolstering long-term prospects. However, declining iPhone sales and market saturation pose risks to top-line growth, making diversification and sustained service performance crucial.

data.title: NuScale: A Nuclear Option For The AI Gold Rush
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NuScale Power Corporation (NYSE:SMR) is a highly speculative investment. In fact, I believe that it's substantially more speculative than Oklo (OKLO). For my part, I've invested in Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) and Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU). I run a concentrated portfolio, and I believe that I already have enough exposure to this overarching theme.

Next, the bull case for NuScale Power is rather straightforward. A narrative that everyone can get behind. A simple narrative. More specifically, the slew of news flows from hyperscalers this year all have one thing in common. They all need nuclear power for their AI data centers.

The bear case here is twofold. The business is burning substantial free cash flow, and NuScale doesn't hold that much cash. And then, there's the inconvenient truth that its stock isn't that cheap.

And yet, I believe that when it comes down to it, the bull case is stronger than the bear case. Therefore, I'm bullish on SMR.

Why NuScale Power? Why Now?

NuScale provides Small Modular Reactors ("SMRs"), a type of nuclear power technology. SMRs are smaller and more flexible than traditional nuclear reactors, making them easier to scale.

NuScale's SMRs generate cheap, reliable energy that's always on, and carbon-free. And how do politicians love this green story? This bull case practically writes itself.

Unlike other SMRs still in the design phase, NuScale's technology is further along, having already received design certification from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission. This gives it an advantage against Oklo in the short term.

I won't regurgitate all the stories from the past week, as anyone following this space already knows them.

What I would argue here is that the bull thesis has a second layer here, that many investors are dying to get indirect exposure to the AI theme, that doesn't involve just backing Nvidia (NVDA). What's the second derivate of the AI gold rush?

As US AI data center electricity demand rises, there's growing pressure for cheap, reliable, scalable, carbon-free energy. And NuScale's SMRs are well-positioned to meet this demand.

Revenue Growth Rates are Difficult to Price

The graphic above is a reminder that NuScale's revenues can abruptly move lower, despite Wall Street's recently found infatuation with Small Modular Reactors.

Case in point, during Q2 2024, the loss of the CFPP LLC contract, saw revenues drop dramatically in the quarter to just $1 million from nearly $6 million in the prior year.

What's more, as you can see above, for NuScale to grow its revenues by any amount, it must incur substantial operating losses, as it prepares itself for strong growth rates in 2025-2026 and beyond.

Next, let's discuss some vague ideas about its valuation.

SMR Stock Valuation - +20x Forward Sales

NuScale holds about $130 million of cash and cash equivalents and no debt. This means that less than 5% of its market cap is made up of cash. As a point of reference, I estimate that Oklo has about 15% of its market cap as cash and cash equivalents and no debt.

What's more, I estimate that NuScale is burning about $150 million of free cash flow per year right now. Potentially more. Therefore, this means that NuScale is going to have to raise some cash. No, a lot of cash. And rapid.

Even though from a purely speculative perspective, I don't believe these details matter. I know it sounds wrong to say this out loud, but flows are flows.

Meaning that the tide raises all boats, and there's a massive difference between trying to feel smart and right and making money. And too many people struggle to know what they want. Generally, people don't like to hear these facts.

On the other hand, even if NuScale needs to raise $300 million, right now, with the number of investors seeking exposure to NuScale's stock, this would dilute shareholders by less than 10%. That's truly rather minimal when we consider just how big this opportunity could turn out to be in 2026 and beyond.

Furthermore, management does have some skin in the game here, which means they are highly incentivized to not dilute themselves too aggressively.

As you can see above, management holds just less than 4% of the stock, meaning that there's more than $100 million worth of equity on the line.

As a reference point, Oklo's management team has more than 33% of ownership in the company. Nearly 10 times more skin in the game, including Wall Street's favorite, Sam Altman.

And as a broad reference point, I'll highlight that NuScale isn't "that cheap," see below.

The graphic above isn't entirely accurate because nobody knows what sort of revenues NuScale could see in 2025. It's just a very broad approximation.

Furthermore, I'm not saying that NuScale competes with Centrus Energy (LEU). Centrus Energy provides the enriched uranium that NuScale and Oklo might use. They are in the same uranium supply chain, but Centrus Energy is further downstream.

Here's a quote from Cameco's (CCJ) recent conference call echoing this thesis.

Remember, in the nuclear fuel cycle, fuel buyers typically don't start with uranium. They actually start downstream. They start with their inventory position and then they start with their fabricating service and then they go to enrichment, then they go to conversion.

[...] the focus is more downstream at the moment. And we see it in really strong enrichment prices and historic conversion prices. So let's just say it's not unusual that the demand hasn't fully found its way into uranium yet. It's usually the last piece of the puzzle.

Essentially, I believe that traders are slightly ahead of themselves right now, when it comes to pricing in how strong the revenue growth rate from NuScale is likely to be. But at the same time, I recognize that in the market where there's a grain of truth, there's belief.

The same happened during the tech bubble of 2000. Then, investors had the right idea that the Internet would change the world, but how exactly it played out, in the end, was rather different from how investors were seeking exposure at the time. They were chasing Yahoo (owned by Apollo Asset Management) and other famous names from history books.

The Bottom Line

NuScale Power stands at the intersection of two major global trends-AI and clean energy.

As data centers grow and demand for uninterrupted power soars, NuScale's SMR technology offers a solution that is both reliable and carbon-free.

While the financial challenges are significant, the potential for long-term success outweighs the risks. I believe we're on the cusp of a new energy era, and traders are chasing not just uranium, but the gold rush of the future.

data.title: Walgreens: Cautious Optimism After Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis/Overview

Two months ago, I pitched a short on Walgreens Boots Alliance (NASDAQ:WBA) on a 1-2 year time horizon, which surprisingly hit my price target in a much shorter time span. Since then, there has been little downside activity in the lead-up to Q4 earnings. After seeing the proper decision-making and clear prioritization from management on the call, I think the bottom is in on WBA. I am upgrading my rating to a Hold, subject to change after an in-depth review of WBA's financials upon the release of the company's 10-K.

Background

My initial article gave a price target of $8.35 for WBA. On September 25th, I exited my short position after WBA closed at $8.25 below that target. The numbers in the screenshot below are not the exact numbers that I had (fees, market order fills, etc.) but they are close enough.

Since that September low, the market has largely traded sideways, with some bull pressure. I have elected to sit on the sidelines waiting to see what would happen after earnings on October 15th, at which point I would do further research on the stock based on management plans and update my rating if necessary. The following section details my thought process during these weeks and what I was looking for from the call, followed by what actually happened in the aftermath.

Key Questions

Going into the earnings release itself on the 15th, I had many questions and points I was considering, and I figured that management would address them on the earnings call.

All the key drivers are still in motion - revenue is steadily declining, there are still retail margin issues, and the company's international segment is growing but does not mean much relative to the core retail pharmacy business. These are all points that I discussed at length in my previous article, and everyone surrounding WBA has the same question - what is management going to do about it? There are multiple ways in which management can do so:

  • Continuing to make attempts to finding its niche, such as by releasing new SKUs
  • Sale of Boots/Cencora/VillageMD to raise cash
  • Closing stores - this last point in particular was something that a lot of people were speculating on, especially because CVS and Rite Aid were closing significant numbers of stores themselves
  • Suspending or cutting the dividend and doing something with the extra cash, although the market reaction to this could be very different from management expectations
  • Trying to fix margin issues by reworking store operating policies and negotiating with PBMs

To me, WBA needs to implement multiple initiatives to really fix the situation, and I wanted to see what management had to say about all of these considerations. Other minor ideas I was thinking about were:

  • Is there a world where the short interest gets so high that WBA becomes a meme stock, given that it was at 10% in the week before earnings? At the moment this is not a substantial risk as GameStop (GME) was more than 100% short in 2020, but if the dominoes fall in the correct order, then this could be something important to the stock.

  • If earnings sentiment turns out to be very poor, will the market really continue to push WBA down when it is sitting around a 3x P/E? It does not seem prudent to keep doing so even if the situation remains dire for the company.

  • How do rate cuts play into this? Now that the Fed has implemented the "jumbo" 50bps cut and will almost certainly cut by another 25 points in November (graph seen below), could this actually help the company? I previously wrote that I expected this to have a marginally beneficial effect, but management could have something to say about refinancing or something related.

End of Short-Run Bearish Sentiment

Perhaps most importantly, I did not really emphasize in my previous article how I felt about the WBA short look. I expected the bearish scenario to play out over a 1-2 year time horizon, which is also the case for the rest of my "initial coverage" SA pitches. (I did not expect my price target to get hit in a month and a half - the intent was for this to be a long-term, slower price downtrend, but I am obviously more than okay with the stock hitting my target sooner rather than later.) This WBA follow-up article happens to be my first "continuing coverage" article, so I will have to see what system or time horizon I will implement with these.

In hindsight, I definitely should have emphasized this in the actual article, as I got a lot of comments on the validity of shorting WBA. I then had to bring up my idea of a short-term decline and then a long-term rally in the comments section, which is far from ideal. This is not to discourage comments or commenters - I appreciate any thoughts/feedback on my writing - but this is something I should have devoted a paragraph to in the actual article. In any case, the quote below is directly from one such comment, and does a decent job summarizing my outlook on WBA:

I agree with you that in the long run, WBA has a good chance of recovering. I think the company could stabilize and get its profit margin trending up again at some point in the future, if the company sheds the right assets. However, I think we will see more downside before this happens. I would not be surprised to see WBA shares up in 5 or 10 years from now - I just think it will get to $8 before then.

Of course, the market did end up selling to $8, and has traded sideways since then. This lack of continued bearish activity has signaled to me that bears do not want to sell at a 3x P/E and that the more extreme Rite Aid-style bankruptcy sentiment is probably very overblown.

This takes me to the last question I was looking for answers to on the 15th - given that bankruptcy concerns are no longer driving the stock into the ground, could WBA shares start to turn around purely off a change in sentiment alone? My August idea that I outlined above was that if management makes the right choices and returns to stability, WBA shares would be a potential deep value long. However, after not seeing much bearish activity following my price target being hit, I was inclined to think that a change in sentiment alone could be enough to start a long-term rally.

In the last article's comments section, I gave an example of a "back from the brink" company in Carvana (CVNA), which currently trades at $190 after being in single-digit territory at the start of 2023, driven via a business turnaround and reverse in sentiment around the company's ability to pay down its debt. I think this is a plausible scenario for WBA, and this would be the most important idea that I would be looking for in the earnings call - just any shift in management's mindset around the company that could bring about a rally.

In summary, for the earnings call, I wanted to focus on:

  • Management's broad plan to turn around the business - any themes or big ideas

  • Improving retail margins - PBM negotiations or store operating costs. This would help WBA's bottom line without selling assets

  • Plans for store closures/other asset liquidation plans if management decides to go down this road

  • Dividend cut/suspension if any, and what management plans to do with extra cash

  • How all of this would affect guidance/sentiment on WBA's top and bottom lines to drive a potential rally

  • Market reaction to earnings - if bearish sentiment below $8.35 is still a possibility, even as this has shown to not be the case over the past month

I was confident that if management discussed all of these points and their ideas reflected positively on a turnaround, the bottom would be in. Should management remain more oblivious to their situation and simply aim to keep the status quo, I was ready to start preparing my "Rite Aid repeat" waterfall analysis and other materials.

Earnings Takeaways & Effect on Outlook

The 15th came and went, and nearly all of my concerns for a turnaround were addressed in some way or form on the call. The earnings transcript is linked here, along with the associated presentation.

  • WBA beat estimates on both EPS and revenue - always a good sign - and FY25 guidance was roughly the same as this year's actual numbers, which is great news given that the company is clearly in a turnaround rather than growth scenario.

  • One of the first things, CEO Tim Wentworth says, is that "WBA is reorienting to its legacy strength as a retail pharmacy-led company." I mentioned in my last article that WBA's international segment is not doing as well as it could be, and WBA's healthcare segment has actually lost money since inception. Having the CEO say that the company should focus on retail pharmacy is good for a turnaround.

  • Wentworth also mentioned that they are in the process of negotiating with PBMs to improve margins, another area for the company to improve COGS.

  • Wentworth also mentioned the company plans to close 1,200 unprofitable stores, thereby improving the company's bottom line. I would strongly prefer improving margins while keeping stores open to avoid "pharmacy deserts," but this might not be practical - in any case, this will definitely help the company from a balance sheet standpoint.

  • Finally, although there was no concrete news on a dividend cut or suspension, Wentworth did not seem entirely opposed to this possibility:

We will absolutely continue to monitor and make changes to our capital allocation, including better aligning our dividend with our long-term earnings power if we believe that that's the appropriate thing to do.

  • WBA shares rocketed up 15% the day of the earnings release, indicating that the bearish sentiment is mostly gone and also in line with my broader takeaway from the call.

There is a case to be made that this could be a one-off situation where, for the moment, all of the sentiment has been reversed, but reality would simply bite the company back later. However, my immediate thoughts after the call were overwhelmingly positive: management demonstrated a clear commitment to retail pharmacy and outlined concrete plans for the near future. Despite some more "bearish facts" around their performance, I feel that this commitment to sizing down to the core shows that the company is ready to change. Furthermore, they are implementing these changes in a way that I really feel will drive a turnaround, as they touched on the vast majority of the points that I posited previously. As such, I think that things only get better from here for the stock, and I think that the bottom is in on WBA.

I do not have a set-in-stone price target for now, but I will take a deeper look at everything after the release of the company's 10-K. In any case, my first impression after earnings is that the company is finally starting to turn around again - more than good enough to upgrade the company to a Hold rating for me. I can also comfortably say now that I think people starting to buy shares at $9 and $10 are making a sensible investment, but I am not ready to fully assign a Buy rating without a proper price target.

Conclusion

I assigned a target of $8.35 to WBA shares two months ago. Once that target got hit, I was split between a Rite Aid Chapter 11 bankruptcy repeat and a rebound similar to that of Carvana stock, and I wanted to wait for this earnings release before modeling for a bankruptcy scenario or calling the bottom. However, after this earnings release checked most of the boxes I was looking for, I think the bottom is in and will be upgrading my rating to a Hold. I will wait for the release of the 10-K to go through the in-depth financials, gauge the broader economic conditions for 2025, and derive a new price target (the lack of this price target is why I am not double-upgrading to a Buy), but I think we will not see $8 or $9 shares anymore.

data.title: Readers Identify 25 Ideal September Dividend Buys Out Of 39 Picks
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author is fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It's possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Dog catching is, by method, a contrarian investing strategy, which is known go against current popular investment thinking. New buyers find it useful, however, as a guide to purchasing dogs on the dips. These lists are suggestions (or warning) advisories, not recommendations.

Reader comments catch errors in calculations or changes in direction. Some readers, for example missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles in the free pages. The “safer” lists are still available to members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group.

A reader in June 2022 alleged that high yields were sure signs of Rogueishness. Not true, I replied. Elevated yield depends on shareprice and corporate direction. High beta is the most accurate measure of Rogueishness.

Reader suggestions of buy and hold dividend stocks are most welcome and sure to be reported on my ReFaRo posts. That’s my ultimate goal, of course, to find 10 or 20 sure fire long-term dividend payers purchased when prices are lower than the dividend from $1K invested. All suggestions are welcome here.

A February 2022 reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

Many readers criticize my favoritism for low-priced stocks. My "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price are especially reviled.

For example, Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI), the satellite radio and Pandora music catalog owner, spun off from Liberty Media and reverse split-priced in October 2024 at $27.10, still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 3.95%. A little over $1k investment now buys 37 shares. And they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) nearly 1.5 times the share price. Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 39 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services survive that long).

Last year, in December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10,000 invested in Apple (AAPL) could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys Apple for the dividend, just growth.

However, I was reminded by a comment in May 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. If the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3% the dividend paid from $1k invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

The dogcatcher ideal balance point is a sweet spot to use for reference. A rule of thumb, on the scale, so to speak.

Every month readers grumble that they can’t find my 10 Dogs of the Week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Investing Groups site.

SA now lists all postings on my Dogcatcher investing group by date.

Here's the guide:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024, and post its last return on October 3, 2025.

My latest Attention (DOTW X) portfolio launched October 11, 2024 and will see all its selection in place by Oct. 10, 2025, and post its last return in October 2026.

Last winter, a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here's my summary of those codes which apply to ll the charts I draw:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

According to reader feedback, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

Foreword

Note that in September 2024 readers mentioned 25 stocks whose dividends from a $1K investment exceeded their single share prices. These are listed below by yield:

The ReFa/Ro September Ideal Dividend Dogs

Above are the 25 ideal candidates derived from the 39 tangible results from reader favorite and rogue equities received prior to September 30, 2024. YCharts data for this article was collected as of 10/14/24.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Estimated 17.99% To 38.19% Net Gains From 10 ReFa/Ro Stocks To September 2025

Six reader favored top-yield August stocks were verified as being among the top 10 gainers for the coming year based on analyst one-year target-prices. (They're tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, this yield-based forecast for reader-fave stocks, as graded by Wall Street wizards, was deemed 60% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1k invested in each of the highest yielding stocks, plus the median one-year analyst target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2024-25 data points which identified probable profit-generating trades. (Note: one-year target prices from lone analysts were not counted.) Thus, 10 probable top August profit-generating trades projected as-of September 14, 2025, were:

Orchid Island Capital (ORC) netted $381.86 based on the median of target price estimates from four analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 86% over the market as a whole. A Rogue.

Ready Capital Corp (RC) was projected to net $380.82 based on the median of target price estimates from six analysts, plus the projected annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% greater than the market as a whole. A Rogue.

Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD) was projected to net $371.43 based on the median of target price estimates from three analysts, plus the projected annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% under the market as a whole. A Fave.

Triple Point Venture Growth BDC Co (TPVG) was projected to net $355.64, based on the median of target price estimates from six analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 80% greater than the market as a whole. TPVG is a Rogue.

Trinity Capital Inc (TRIN) netted $262.73 based the median of target price estimates from eight analysts, plus the projected annual dividend, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for TRIN. It is a Rogue.

Generation Income Properties Inc (GIPR) netted $221.68 based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for GIPR. It is a Rogue.

Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI) was projected to net $214.06, based on the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 7% over the market as a whole. A Rogue.

Pfizer Inc (PFE) was projected to net $199.77, based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 32% under the market as a whole. A Fave.

Eagle Point Credit Co Inc (ECC) was projected to net $188.20, based on the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 7% under the market as a whole. It's a Fave.

data.title: Bank of America: Q3 Points To Upside Breakout (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) reported better-than-expected results for its third quarter thanks to higher investment banking fees and trading revenues.

With that said, there is also a potential cause for concern as the bank suffered an increase in loan losses, resulting in a somewhat softer credit profile.

With inflation moderating and the U.S. economy being arguably in resilient shape, I think that Bank of America has a lot of potential to deliver positive surprises if credit issues remain under control.

The stock showed an inclination for an upside breakout after 3Q24 earnings, and the technical chart profile looks compelling as well.

My Rating History

My last stock classification for Bank of America's stock was Buy in no small part because the bank profited from a higher-for-longer rate environment.

Moreover, Bank of America's net interest income margin was healthy, and the bank guided for growth in its net interest income in 2024, despite the expectation of lower benchmark interest rates.

Trading and investment banking did particularly well in the third quarter and Bank of America once again produced billions of profits for shareholders.

Robust 3Q24 Profits, But Loan Quality Potentially An Issue

Bank of America's total sales, net of interest expenses, were up 1% YoY to $25.3 billion in 3Q24. Helpful in the third quarter were primarily higher investment banking revenues and asset management fees which aided Bank of America's 3Q24 results: The bank produced $6.9 billion in profits in the third quarter which was the same amount earned in the prior quarter.

Asset management fees rose 14% compared to the year ago period amid market strength which is also what helped the bank log 18% higher investment banking fees in the third quarter.

Still, profits declined 12% YoY and a major factor here were higher credit losses which went up $300 million compared to the same period a year ago to $1.5 billion. Credit provisions thus increased 25% YoY while net-charge offs increased 65% YoY. Total net-charge offs were also $1.5 billion, about the same as they were in 2Q24, but investors definitely want to keep an eye here on how the trend develops moving forward.

Higher credit losses are a potential cause for concern if the U.S. economy slips into a recession which I don't consider to be a realistic near-term scenario. The jobs report in September showed healthy job growth, with the U.S. economy adding 254K new jobs, beating the consensus of 150K jobs by a mile.

In 3Q24, Bank of America also earned $14.1 billion in net interest income, reflecting an increase of $200 million QoQ. The bank forecasted to grow its net interest income to $14.5 billion by 4Q24, primarily because of a positive growth outlook for mortgage and auto loans.

Bank of America's net interest income decreased $400 million YoY, however, in light of higher deposit costs that made a bigger impact than incrementally higher asset yields.

Technical Chart Profile

Technically, Bank of America is in a long-term up-channel with the stock breaking out above the 200-day moving average line in November of last year.

Since then, BAC has remained above this trendline and completed a textbook bounce-off in early August when the stock market temporarily melted down.

Just now, with the help of the bank's 3Q24 results, Bank of America also broke out above the 20-day and 50-day moving average lines which are short-term oriented trend indicators. The technical picture here is very bullish, and I would not be surprised to see new highs being achieved imminently.

Still Cheap, Possible Upside

Bank of America's book value as of September 30, 2024, was $35.37 per share, up 8.3% YoY thanks to robust growth across the bank's businesses, particularly consumer and investment banking.

Bank of America's stock is thus selling for a 21% premium to book value whereas Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) is selling for a 32% premium. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) is selling for a 94% premium for book value.

Because Bank of America is still so enormously profitable and growing its book value easily, I think the bank has ongoing rerating potential: Taking into account the cyclical support that Bank of America has from a robust labor market and a growing U.S. economy, I think a 30% premium to book value remains an appropriate mark-up for the bank.

By the end of the year, based on present growth rates, Bank of America could have a book of $37-38 per share which equates to an intrinsic value of $48-49.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Pan Out

Bank of America owns a considerable amount of floating-rate assets (loans) on its balance sheet that in the long run are poised to produce lower interest income for the bank if we go along with the assumption that the central bank will slash benchmark interest rates in 2024 and particularly in 2025.

That said, Bank of America is broadly positioned with its multiservice bank model, serving both consumers and corporations.

I think it would be a good idea to pay some attention to the credit profile moving forward, as any evolving weakness in the U.S. economy would probably show up here early.

My Conclusion

Bank of America had a robust third quarter: The bank was widely profitable, profited from momentum in investment banking and asset management, and managed to grow its net interest income and book value.

There were some minor issues regarding rising credit losses (higher provisions) as well, but I think that the earnings release in its entirety remained favorable for Bank of America.

The stock has just broken out above the 20-day and 50-day moving average lines, on growing volumes, indicating a positive change in sentiment that, in my view, could last as long as the U.S. economy adds jobs and inflation retreats. Buy.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Why I'm Buying More Shares
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) shares experienced weakness at the start of last month and have been trying to regain their momentum ever since. Despite this, I believe that Warren Buffett's firm remains a solid investment, especially at the current price. A more favorable macro environment should help Berkshire Hathaway's insurance business and investing portfolio perform better in the foreseeable future and create additional shareholder value along the way.

At the same time, after unwinding a significant portion of Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) shares in recent quarters, I also believe that Berkshire Hathaway has become an even better investment. While there are nevertheless several risks that could undermine Berkshire Hathaway's growth opportunities, I still believe that in the current environment, the firm remains a solid BUY, and I'm buying more shares.

The Growth Story Is Not Over Yet

Since the publication of my latest bullish article on Berkshire Hathaway in March, its shares have appreciated by ~10% and have all the chances to continue to appreciate in the long run. If we take a closer look at the firm's earnings report for Q2 which was released a couple of months ago, we'll see that Berkshire Hathaway showed a solid performance as its operating earnings increased by 15.5% Y/Y to $11.60 billion. What's more impressive is that Berkshire Hathaway ended the quarter with $276.94 billion in cash reserves, which indicates that it has more than enough resources to invest in new growth opportunities and ensure that the firm continues to expand and create additional shareholder value for years to come.

The Q3 earnings report is expected to be released in early November, and it's safe to assume that the firm will report a relatively decent performance. After all, the firm's insurance business thrived in Q2 as the underwriting earnings were up 81.5% Y/Y to $2.26 billion, while the insurance investment income was up 40% Y/Y to $3.32 billion.

One of the reasons behind such a performance was the fact that no significant catastrophe events happened during the reporting period, which greatly benefitted the insurance business. While in late Q3 there was Hurricane Helene and in Q4 there was Hurricane Milton, it's safe to say that the worst-case scenario has been avoided and the performance of the insurance business should not be as bad as someone might expect. At the same time, as the United States is expected to continue to generate almost half of the globe's insurance premiums, while the global insurance spending is forecasted to reach almost $10 trillion by 2028, it's safe to assume that Berkshire Hathaway's insurance business will continue to thrive.

What's more is that in case the insurance business underperforms in Q3, Berkshire Hathaway will likely be able to offset the potential lost earnings thanks to the potentially higher investment income during the period given that the firm has been actively selling its stake in Bank of America Corporation (BAC) in recent months. Given that Warren Buffett invested in the financial institution in 2011 when it was trading in single digits, it's safe to say that selling at the current market price should greatly improve Berkshire Hathaway's investment income in Q3 and Q4.

On top of all of that, I view Berkshire Hathaway's trimming of Apple's stake in recent quarters as a positive development. As is the case with Bank of America, Berkshire Hathaway already made a great return on its investment in Apple and probably at the right time as well. In my recent article on Apple, I noted that the declining sales in China and the inability to launch Apple Intelligence in China and the European Union will probably limit Apple's upside in the upcoming quarters. Therefore, Berkshire Hathaway had a good exit point in my opinion. However, if my thesis on Apple doesn't play out, then Berkshire Hathaway would nevertheless benefit from this as it's still holding a decent position in the business.

As such, I believe that Berkshire Hathaway is a much better investment than Apple since the firm will benefit from the potential further appreciation of Apple's shares. At the same time, it won't be risking losing all of its capital if my thesis plays out since it already sold a significant portion of its stake at a great profit earlier this year, while its other investments will likely offset the downside.

In addition to all of that, the better-than-expected growth of the American economy coupled with significant cash reserves give Berkshire Hathaway more than enough flexibility to deploy capital for new growth ideas. The firm has already increased its stake in Sirius XM Holdings Inc. (SIRI), which appears to be undervalued at a P/E of ~10x and has a cash-generating potential as its profitability is greater in comparison to the sector's median. At the same time, by raising additional funds via Yen-denominated bonds recently, Berkshire Hathaway has more than enough flexibility to deploy capital in various markets to continue to invest in growth opportunities.

Is Berkshire Hathaway a Buy?

Considering everything stated above, it's safe to assume that Berkshire Hathaway remains a decent investment right now. The firm has a GAAP P/E (TTM) of ~15x and a non-GAAP P/E (TTM) of ~24x, while the average P/E of the companies within the S&P 500 Index is ~30x, which indicates that Berkshire Hathaway is a much better firm to own for value investors since it appears to be undervalued.

At the same time, Berkshire Hathaway's five biggest public holdings all have a rating of A+ for profitability, which suggests that all of them are performing great in the current environment, have solid balance sheets, and rely less on borrowing costs to grow their businesses. This makes it possible to assume that Berkshire Hathaway's investing portfolio is relatively safe, and its biggest holdings are unlikely to face any unexpected events that could undermine its business models, which is another major upside for the overall firm.

On top of all of that, Berkshire Hathaway doesn't have direct competitors due to its unique structure and size. Given that the company is trading close to the $1 trillion market cap, it makes more sense to compare it to the market's biggest firms to figure out Berkshire Hathaway's investment attractiveness.

The table below shows that Berkshire Hathaway has much more cash reserves in comparison to the biggest names in the market, which gives it more flexibility in deciding where to deploy its capital to create additional shareholder value. At the same time, Berkshire Hathaway also trades at lower multiples, which indicates that its stock at the current price could be considered a much better investment than Big Tech names in this environment.

The consensus on the street is that Berkshire Hathaway still represents an upside at the current price, which is another reason to be optimistic about its future.

In addition to that, Seeking Alpha's Quant system gives Berkshire Hathaway stock a Strong Buy recommendation and ranks it at #30 out of over 4000 stocks that it covers. As such, I continue to believe that Berkshire Hathaway is a great value investment at the current price and its stock has all the chances to continue to appreciate to higher levels in the foreseeable future.

Major Risks To Bullish Thesis

While there are plenty of growth opportunities, Berkshire Hathaway nevertheless faces several risks that could undermine its growth in the following quarters. Despite the stellar performance of the insurance business in Q2, there's still a risk that it will underperform in the short term due to the costs associated with the recent hurricanes in the United States.

At the same time, the changing regulatory climate could also result in mixed performance for the firm's rail and energy businesses. Both of them underperformed in Q2 and that could be the case in Q3 and Q4 considering that Warren Buffett noted earlier this year that government regulations are hurting some businesses in which Berkshire Hathaway is a major shareholder.

In addition to all of that, the fact that Berkshire Hathaway is buying fewer of its shares while Warren Buffett himself is unlikely to run the firm for much longer due to his age could make some investors hesitant to buy the shares at this stage. This is due to the fact that it's hard to predict whether Berkshire Hathaway's successors will be able to match the performance of the Oracle of Omaha himself over the long term.

The Bottom Line

While several risks could undermine Berkshire Hathaway's growth story, its stock at the current price still appears to be a great investment for value investors. The firm managed to realize some portion of profits from its investment in Apple at relatively high prices, which helped it create significant shareholder value by achieving record operating earnings. At the same time, Berkshire Hathaway continues to have a decent exposure to Apple, which should help it continue to benefit from the firm's potential growth in the future.

On top of that, by having a significant cash pile and continuing to hold stakes in great businesses, Berkshire Hathaway has everything going for it to continue to create additional shareholder value in years to come. Therefore, the growth story is still not over yet and the firm's stock remains a BUY for me.

data.title: Nvidia's Blackwell Can't Defy Gravity: A Major Decline Is Coming
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) might be the stock of the moment—growing almost 17% since my last Hold rating—but momentum can't defy gravity. I think the stock is due for a big crash in the medium term when the demand for training GPUs significantly tapers. This could lead to a big revenue contraction for Nvidia. Even though in the next two to three years revenue growth looks set to continue (at a slowing pace), in the distant horizon, the dynamics of the AI compute market show signs of trouble for Nvidia shareholders.

My Nvidia rating is currently still a Hold, but someday soon, in the next year or so, I'm sure it will be a Sell. Here are the main reasons why.

Blackwell Is Showing Unprecedented Demand, but It Can’t Defy Gravity

Nvidia's Blackwell GPUs have sparked unprecedented demand, with supplies for the next 12 months completely sold out. This doesn't change my Hold thesis, but it does set the stage for what could be a period of sustained growth (albeit more moderate than what we saw in 2024) through 2025. Major tech companies like AWS (AMZN), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Oracle (ORCL) have pre-purchased all available Blackwell GPUs for the next year.

Nvidia is competing with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which has released its Instinct MI325X AI chip (which management claims outranks Nvidia's H200 processor in specific AI workloads). It has plans to release the MI350 series (to compete with Nvidia's Blackwell) in 2025. However, the demand for Nvidia's products and its dominant position in the market puts it far ahead of AMD, which has carved a market position related to cost competition rather than world-leading performance.

The high demand for AI training chips currently is due to the AI arms race. It is a major consideration that the group demand for AI GPUs is likely to remain unabated until all Big Tech companies begin to slow down in tandem. The Magnificent Seven need to collaborate as well as compete in this regard. Too heavy an emphasis on “being the best, the fastest” could spur herd buying behavior among the cohort, leading to Western financial inefficiencies and poor returns on investment in our flagship tech industry.

That being said, in the interim, Nvidia seems set to continue growing quite robustly. The company expects to generate several billion dollars in revenue from Blackwell in the fourth quarter of 2024, with continued demand into 2025. Nvidia currently controls approximately 90% of the market for advanced AI chips, and this development further positions it as a growing monopoly despite the competition from AMD.

Nvidia has a history of capitalizing on emerging trends, and it always shows a slight dip in revenue following this. I expect it to be no different with the AI infrastructure build-out. The question is whether sentiment in the market will remain strong as the company transitions from its high-revenue generating AI training era toward a slower growth period of AI inference and robotics.

Despite all the Strong Buy ratings on Wall Street, I still question the long-term (five-to-10-year) validity of investing in the AI arms race through Nvidia. One may be able to get one to two more years of strong returns from this investment, but the stock price is likely to become volatile as its revenues really begin to slow.

As the training phase plateaus because models are largely fully trained, the demand for GPUs is likely to decrease as fewer resources are needed for ongoing model development. The inference phase then uses trained models to make predictions or decisions—the need for efficiency in these computations then becomes desirable; therefore, the market for GPUs optimized for inference will take precedence over training GPUs.

Nvidia has positioned itself well already for inference tasks, including through its TensorRT platform. It has built an ecosystem including software tools and libraries that support both training and inference, and I expect this (along with robotics) will be management's core focus in 2027 and beyond.

The main reason this could be problematic for Nvidia shareholders in the medium term is that the training phase requires a high initial spend, whereas spending on inference GPUs is more spread out over time. As AI applications become more widely integrated into industries, the demand for inference will grow steadily. Therefore, we can expect a significant reduction in the revenue growth rates of Nvidia once the AI infrastructure build-out has ended. In fact, I think we could even see a major revenue decline. This is when the stock could show signs of a “Cisco (CSCO) moment.”

My rating is still a Hold because the AI arms race has not culminated. However, Nvidia shareholders are in very risky territory, given my analysis here. In my opinion, Nvidia should now be no more than 5% of portfolios. I can see a valid reason to sell at the present valuation and price. However, we likely have two or three more years of steady training-based growth before this calamity could occur. I think the question is when the market picks up on this at large, not if. When it does, we could see an Nvidia “crash.” That could be in three, four, or five years.

Valuation Update: The Stock Will Continue To Climb (For Now)

Currently, Nvidia has a forward P/E non-GAAP ratio of 46.3. That's fine to my mind, and likely somewhat sustainable over the next couple of years. I'm quite certain that demand for Nvidia's training GPUs will remain robust in 2025 (especially as macroeconomic conditions become more favorable with a lower interest rate environment). However, I have mentioned in my publications that I believe we could enter a more troubling period of macroeconomic weakness (including potential stagflation) if interest rates are not kept moderate in 2025 and inflation issues become worse. 2026 and 2027 could be vulnerable years for Big Tech stocks, especially as geopolitical tensions could intensify at this time. I estimate Nvidia will achieve 50% normalized EPS growth in 2025. I also estimate that it will achieve approximately 49% revenue growth. Already, this is a significant contraction from the year-over-year earnings growth of 125% and year-over-year revenue growth of 109%, which I expect it to deliver for 2024—hence my Hold rating.

Nvidia Tesla Amazon
2025 Normalized EPS Growth Estimate 50% 40% 25%

Based on the above peer analysis with other high-growth Big Tech peers, Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon, Nvidia looks approximately fairly valued to me—for now. However, just remember what happened to Tesla when it experienced a revenue contraction—hint: Nvidia's 'Cisco moment'. Tesla's revenue decline was only moderate, but look at how significant the effect was on its stock price.

The good news is that Tesla is likely to have another long growth runway related to humanoid robots and autonomous taxis. My prediction is that in the upcoming years, Nvidia's stock will crash, but it will also regain ground related to inference, robotics, and other advanced technology opportunities. That being said, Nvidia's big golden win as the dominant player in training GPUs is not a multi-decade opportunity. Nvidia got lucky and has done remarkably well to consolidate its position in a disciplined fashion, but its luck (or should I say the opportunity) won't last forever.

Currently, my base-case 12-month price target for Nvidia is a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 35 and a full-year 2025 normalized EPS of $4.39. That would lead to a stock price of $153.65. This is a 13% increase from the present price of $135.70. If the market continues to look favorably on Nvidia and signs of revenue contraction don't start to manifest in the mainstream more clearly, we could see it trade at a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 40+; this would lead to a stock price of over $175, a price return of 29% or more from the present $135.70.

Conclusion

The points in this thesis should be crystal clear to long-term-oriented investors. I am currently not an Nvidia stakeholder, but if I were, I wouldn't be holding the stock at more than 5% of my portfolio right now. I want to hold any investments I make for five, 10, or 15 years. Nvidia's best growth days are largely behind it. Now, we're going to see slowing growth rates, and then, in a few years, I predict a revenue contraction. That's all going to be because the one-off big revenue opportunity related to high AI training GPU demand for the AI arms race will taper.

data.title: UTG: Running Hot But Still An Attractive Long-Term Choice
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Reaves Utility Income Fund (NYSE:UTG) is a closed-end fund invested in a diversified portfolio of primarily utility investments, but also including other infrastructure exposure. The fund boasts an incredibly long history of no distribution cuts since its inception, which was prior to the Global Financial Crisis. This type of stability of its payout with a current positive outlook that it can be maintained makes this fund quite attractive.

Since our last update, the fund has been running higher, significantly higher and has been able to outpace the S&P 500 Index during this time. This was thanks to heading into a period of the Fed cutting their target interest rate, which can help UTG in a couple of different ways.

Though the fund currently trades at only a slight discount, it wouldn't necessarily be considered too richly valued. Relatively speaking, a premium has not been a rare occurrence for UTG over most of the last decade plus. Given the better environment of lower interest rates utilities and UTG are set to continue to perform strongly. I believe that the fund can continue to perform well and it still represents a strong choice for a long-term investor.

UTG Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.35
  • Discount/Premium: -1.41%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.94%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.95%
  • Leverage: 16.96%
  • Managed Assets: $3.214 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

UTG's investment objective is "to provide a high level of after-tax total return, consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation." To achieve this, the fund "intends to invest at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry."

Performance Comparisons And Valuation

One of the comparisons that is often made to UTG is the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), another solid name in the infrastructure CEF space. Over the last three years, the performance of these funds on a total NAV return basis has favored UTF. However, both funds materially lagged the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU).

Given the higher borrowing rates, despite UTF being hedged to a large degree, the funds were weaker when the Fed was raising rates aggressively. Being leveraged meant these funds saw further downside. I'd also want to mention that both UTG and UTF are more diversified compared to XLU, which might not make them the most comparable as there are material differences.

When looking back at the last five years of performance—which still includes the Covid crash—XLU had still come out on top and UTG has been the biggest laggard during this time. UTF was able to keep up a bit more in terms of not letting XLU get too far ahead.

However, things can always change and they can change quickly. Over the last year now, it has been UTG leading the way higher, with that coming out in more recent weeks after the Fed cut their benchmark rate.

UTF has now been the laggard, and this is one of the reasons why I don't mind holding both funds. I believe both are strong long-term positions, but they often take turns outperforming each other over time.

With UTG currently at only a bit of a discount, some may consider it too expensive to consider adding at this time. However, with UTG, the fund often has traded at a premium for quite a few years now. So that isn't anything too out of the ordinary. Even going back to look at the average over the last decade, UTG isn't trading egregiously above its average. That said, one of the main cautions could be that if the fund does ever return to a -10 % discount, which has happened before, that would lead to quite a sizeable drop.

For investors a bit more cautious about that could consider using a dollar-cost average approach and not go all in upfront.

A further consideration is that UTG hadn't hedged its leverage costs, so any reduction in the Fed's short-term rates is going to benefit UTG. In their last semi-annual report, they were paying an interest rate of 5.97%. They pay on a rate of one month SOFR plus 0.65%. With a 50 basis point rate cut from the Fed, those lower borrowing costs are already kicking in, with more expected before year-end.

A lower rate environment also bodes well for the underlying utility and other infrastructure positions in the fund as they have high capex, which is generally going to be covered by issuing more debt. A lower rate environment means they should also be seeing lower borrowing costs. That's also not to mention that a lower risk-free Treasury Rate should see infrastructure companies that are often associated as being income-oriented investments for investors start to look more attractive.

Adding all that up, and we can start to see why UTG has been performing so strongly more recently. I think there is more to go as well.

Steady Distribution

On the distribution front, there never really is too much to discuss with this fund. In fact, it is a rather "boring" fund when it comes to its distribution, but you don't always want a fund that has too much "excitement" when it comes to its payout. So I view that as another positive for the fund as its only raised its distribution since it launched.

At a modest 6.84% NAV distribution rate and the lower costs of borrowings that should free up more net investment income for the fund to fund its distribution, I don't see a lot changing going forward at this time either.

That said, investors should be quite aware that the fund still relies on significant capital gains to cover its distribution. The latest semi-annual report shows NII coverage of 27%.

Even with lower borrowing costs, it is highly unlikely that NII will start covering the entire distribution for investors. They paid $16.266 million interest on their loan, according to the last semi-annual report, a six-month period. At that time, they had borrowings outstanding of $545 million, so that math roughly checks out based on the average interest rate charged 5.99%, coming out to $32.646 million, then divided by 2 to reach $16.323 million.

Assuming a 50 basis point reduction currently to bring their interest rate down to 5.5%, they'll be paying $29.975 million annually. That comes out to a roughly $2.671 million reduction; against the total shares outstanding of ~81.632 million would work out to roughly a $0.032 increase in NII per share for every 50 basis point reduction due to a reduction in borrowing costs.

We know the Fed is expected to cut rates further, but this was just to provide the calculations of potential savings behind it. The exercise was to help illustrate that even if rates went to zero—which isn't likely to happen—UTG would still require capital gains.

Given that the fund fairly frequently trades at a premium, they also have another lever they can pull. We can see from the above that investors have been reinvesting dividends and they've also been selling significant amounts of shares through their at-the-market offering. When selling shares at a premium, that is accretive to NAV. In this case, the premium isn't sizeable enough to actually contribute a calculable amount—meaning it is a fraction of a penny. However, at the same time, it isn't a negative.

Another consideration is that even with a small premium, investors who participate in the fund-sponsored DRIP can get slightly discounted shares. The fund offers a reinvestment at NAV or up to a 5% discount on the market price.

If, on the payment date for any Dividend, the closing market price plus estimated brokerage commissions per Common Share is equal to or greater than the net asset value per Common Share, the Plan Administrator will invest the Dividend amount in Newly Issued Common Shares on behalf of the participants. The number of Newly Issued Common Shares to be credited to each participant’s account will be determined by dividing the dollar amount of the Dividend by the net asset value per Common Share on the payment date; provided that, if the net asset value is less than or equal to 95% of the closing market value on the payment date, the dollar amount of the Dividend will be divided by 95% of the closing market price per Common Share on the payment date. If, on the payment date for any Dividend, the net asset value per Common Share is greater than the closing market value plus estimated brokerage commissions, the Plan Administrator will invest the Dividend amount in Common Shares acquired on behalf of the participants in Open-Market Purchases.

Even at a small premium, it can still work out to "free shares" over time.

The coverage here is pretty consistent with the distribution tax classifications over time, as well, where most years require capital gains to fund its payout. Though, bear in mind that tax classification and actual NII/capital gains realized in the fund aren't always exactly the same.

Interestingly, there were some exceptions around the Global Financial Crisis, where the fund's underlying portfolio. Going back to those years, that actually had been the case as well, and the fund had enough NII to cover the total distributions to investors.

UTG's Portfolio

In general, the fund is weighted toward mostly utilities. That said, communication services, industrials, energy and real estate sectors all carry some fairly meaningful weights in the fund as well. Since our last update, there hasn't been a material change in the weightings.

Another positive for considering distribution coverage improving from NII is not only seeing that increase as borrowing costs come down but also the underlying portfolio. Of course, the underlying portfolio is what ultimately generates the NII as those positions pay dividends to UTG, which get passed out to shareholders of UTG. UTG provides a list of dividend changes every quarter and it is often the case that they see many more dividend boosts than cuts.

At the end of Q2 2024, they noted these stats:

Reaves Utility Income Fund owned 59 common stocks as of 6/30/24, 54 of which paid a cash dividend in Q2 2024. In the past 12 months, 45 increased their dividend, six maintained the same payment, three initiated a dividend, and none reduced the dividend. The weighted average dividend increase was 6.2%.

If the fund can hold operating expenses relatively stable, a 6.2% average dividend increase from the underlying holdings should translate into higher NII for investors at the end of the day. The breakdown below is as of June 30, 2024.

A couple of the largest increases we saw were still some of their largest holdings as well, which include Constellation Energy (CEG) and Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY). Those names are doing some of the heavy lifting, which should help out UTG's NII increase.

The fund also holds a few names that initiated dividends over the last year, one of the more notable being T-Mobile (TMUS). It's a relatively sizeable position for the fund, and even better since this was put together, TMUS also raised its dividend by 35.38% to $0.88 per quarter.

Conclusion

UTG continues to pump out a stable monthly distribution to its investors. The fund regularly trades around parity with its NAV per share, but that isn't out of line with how the fund has been trading for ~five years now. While the fund could drop to a 10% discount and result in some material downside, I believe the better overall environment of lower rates should bode well for UTG. For a more cautious investor who still wants to add or initiate a position in this fund, utilizing a dollar-cost average approach could be a better approach.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Presidential Election: What Investors Need To Know
data.description:
The U.S. presidential election is weeks away, with polls showing a very close race. John Eade, President of Argus Research, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell about the various election result scenarios and what it could mean for markets.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - We're only a few weeks away from the US election, and the outcome may have a big impact on the markets. Joining us now to discuss, John Eade, President of Argus Research. John, great to have you back on the show.

John Eade - Thanks, Greg. Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - It's incredible that we're so deep into the year. Now, the election seemed it was so far away, and now it's practically on our doorstep. I know that your shop has done some work on how the markets usually perform through an election cycle. What does history tell us?

John Eade - So, Greg, there's a real pattern that we typically see during the presidential cycle, but that pattern has been broken a little bit this time around. Typically, here in the US, the president serves for four years. And that first year of their term is a good year for the markets.

There's a lot of enthusiasm for new energy and new ideas. So you get a double-digit gain, generally, in the first year of a presidency. In the second year, the bloom is off the rose a little bit-- maybe some of the policies have failed or didn't get through Congress.

A lot of times, you have changes in the makeup of Congress during the midterm elections. So the second year is a positive year, but only mid-single digits instead of mid-teens. The third year, according to the pattern, is another good year for stocks.

And this makes sense, because by now, the president is already looking forward to re-election, the election in the following year. So there's probably going to be some fiscal stimulus boosting the economy. So the third year is another good year.

And then the fourth year is typically another bad year. Usually, there's a lot of uncertainty about who's going to take over, how many changes there might be, what it'll do to regulation, to taxes, to trade policies. However, this year, we're in a bull market heading into the final few weeks of the year. So this is a year that breaks the pattern a little bit, but in a good way.

Greg Bonnell - All right. Yeah, anyone who's long the market and the pattern is to be broken to the upside, that is a good way. Obviously, we have so much attention, and rightly so, focused on who is going to take the White House, the presidential election. But you mentioned Congress there.

This is important, too. Often, we'll see political gridlock in Washington-- not great for passing policy, but it's considered to be good for the markets, isn't it?

John Eade - It generally is, but it doesn't have to be the only outcome or the only result for stocks. First of all, just a real focused level on who we have running. We can look at the track record for the Trump years, and those were generally good years for stocks-- average gains of about 15% for the four years that Trump was in the presidency.

There were swings, for sure. But on average, they were good years. But the Biden years, and I'm using the Biden years as a proxy, potentially, for Kamala Harris-- the Biden years haven't been bad either, with an average of 12% to 13% gains.

And you look at the 15% gains and the 12% to 13% gains, they're both above the 10% historical average. So you could argue that you've got a decent track record from either candidate coming into the vote early next month.

But, to your specific point about gridlock-- actually, the market-beating returns have come in the few instances where there was a Republican sweep-- so the Republicans controlled the presidency, they controlled the Senate, and they controlled the House of Representatives. Those were, on average, the best years for stocks. So no gridlock there.

But if you just look at the years that a Democrat was a president versus Republican was president, the Democrats outperform. So I think a lot of it, Greg, has to do more with the fundamentals in the market at the time-- what's going on with interest rates? What's going on with the unemployment rate? What's going on with stock market valuations and earnings? I think at the end of the day, that's going to be a better focus than what the composition is of the Congress and the White House in any particular year.

Greg Bonnell - That's important, and great perspective there. We don't know. It is a close race. Let's run down the scenarios. Trump takes the White House. How do you view that for the markets?

John Eade - So if Trump takes the White House, there's a good chance that the Republicans regain control of the Senate. It's very close now. The Vice President has been giving the deciding vote.

So the Republicans probably regain the Senate and hold on to the House. So you have that Republican sweep. And, once again, history shows that that's been a market-beating lineup if you just look at the numbers.

There are some factors, of course, that make Trump unique in many ways. He's very America-First. So that's not going to be great for our trading partners. That's probably going to raise tariffs.

That's not going to help our exporters. And the higher tariffs may ultimately lead to higher inflation. On the other side, Trump is probably going to be focused more on lower taxes. And the lower taxes can, potentially, improve consumer spending.

And then lower taxes at the corporate level can improve corporate profits. So those are some of the high-level points about a Trump presidency.

Greg Bonnell - OK, now let's do a Harris win.

John Eade - So with Harris, the simple response is to say that Harris is going to look a lot like how Biden has governed. However, Harris leans a little bit farther to the left than Biden does. And Harris almost certainly will be facing the kind of gridlock that oftentimes leads to decent market performance.

So probably the most aggressive of any Harris programs won't stand a chance to be passed. I think a lot of what we're going to have here is the status quo with stable tax rates where we are. It's probably fiscal deficits, but I think we get those either way. But I think, Greg, with Harris, it's going to be more of the same like we're seeing today.

Greg Bonnell - Lastly, I want to ask you, John, because it's so close, there's a thought that we're going to go to bed not knowing election night. We're going to wake up the next day, perhaps, not fully knowing.

You go back to Bush versus Gore-- that was early in my career, but I do remember it going on for weeks and weeks, because, of course, the result was contested. What happens then for the markets?

John Eade - So that's probably the worst outcome, Greg. We just reviewed who's better, the Democrat, the Republican. That's pretty much a coin flip. So whatever outcome we get is probably going to be OK for the stock market.

And I think that's what you're seeing with the rally that we've had year-to-date. There aren't any big concerns about one or the other wins. But if the election isn't decided for days, or weeks, or even months, I think that's when the questions are raised about the stability of the system, not to mention the fairness of the outcome.

And that's going to bring a lot of uncertainty into the market. And that's probably going to result in some kind of market sell-off. The good news, though, as you know, this has been a big year for elections around the world-- India, South Africa, France, UK, Mexico-- all of these countries have held elections. They've all ended up with results. The countries have moved on. And I think the US is going to fit that pattern too, ultimately.
